summary:
The Polynesian settlement of Fiji (c. 1000 BC) and the subsequent expansion into western Polynesia (Tonga and Samoa, c. 900–800 BC) represents the founding phase of Polynesian civilization — the eastern branch of Lapita cultural expansion that eventually populated the entire Pacific Ocean as far as Hawaii, Easter Island, and New Zealand. The Lapita people, identified by their distinctive decorated pottery, migrated from Near Oceania (Melanesia) eastward across the open Pacific using sophisticated double-hulled voyaging canoes and navigation skills that allowed them to find and settle islands over distances of thousands of kilometers.

Fiji and Tonga/Samoa became the 'cradle of Polynesia' — the region where the distinctive Polynesian cultural complex developed from Lapita roots over several centuries. The Ancestral Polynesian society that emerged in western Polynesia (by c. 500 BC) was characterized by hereditary chiefly hierarchy (ariki), elaborate ceremonial exchange networks, ancestor veneration, and the maritime expertise that would fuel the later expansion into eastern and then remote Polynesia. This was the most extensive human colonization movement of the ancient world, eventually settling the last major habitable land areas on Earth — the Hawaiian Islands (c. 400 AD), New Zealand (c. 1200 AD), and Easter Island — using only non-instrumental navigation.
